title: 멀미해요
scene: 🚗 드라이브 중 · On a Road Trip

goal.en: I can talk about how I'm feeling physically.

key_points: -아/어지다 — change of state (괜찮아졌어? / 나아진 것 같아)
-게 되다 — came about / ended up (도착하게 돼)
-는 것 명사화 — nominalization (참는 것이 쉽지 않지)
-지 않다 vs -지 못하다 — won't/isn't vs can't

script.speaker: grandma
script.en: Mira, is your stomach feeling any better?

annotation.note:
형용사 + 아/어지다 = become / get [adj]
괜찮아지다 = get better
괜찮아졌어? = Have you gotten better?
예뻐지다 / 좋아지다 / 나아지다

script.speaker: grandma
script.en: Do you think you can hold on a little longer?

script.speaker: grandpa
script.en: We'll be at the rest stop soon.

annotation.grammar: V-게 되다 — end up / come to
annotation.note:
동사 + 게 되다 = end up ~ing (naturally happens)
이제 곧 도착하게 돼 = You'll end up arriving soon
= 자연스럽게 그렇게 됨
알게 됐어요 = I came to know / I found out

script.speaker: mirae
script.en: It's so hard to hold on, Grandpa.

script.speaker: grandpa
script.text: 그래, 멀미를 참는 것이 쉽지 않지.
script.en: I know — fighting off motion sickness isn't easy.

annotation.note:
동사 + 는 것 = the act of / ~ing
멀미를 참는 것이 쉽지 않지
= The act of enduring motion sickness isn't easy
먹는 것 = eating / the thing of eating

script.speaker: grandpa
script.text: 그래도 조금만 더 참아 볼 수 있겠어?
script.en: Can you try to hang in there just a little longer?

script.speaker: lia
script.text: 언니, 우리 휴게소에 가면 시원한 빙수 먹자.
script.en: Sis, let's get some cold bingsu when we get there!

script.speaker: mirae
script.en: Sotteok-sotteok and Korean corn dogs too.

script.speaker: mirae
script.en: I think I'm starting to feel a bit better now.

items.english: to feel motion sick · 차멀미 = carsick / 배멀미 = seasick
to endure / hold on / put up with
to get better / to improve (in health)
highway rest stop · Korea's rest stops are legendary for food!
sotteok-sotteok — rice cake & sausage skewers, glazed with sauce
Korean corn dog — battered & deep-fried (not the American version!)
now / by now / at this point
a bit / somewhat · also softens requests: 좀 도와줘 = give me a hand, will you?
just a little more / a little longer
to seem / to be like · -는 것 같다 = it seems like…

grammar.title: -아/어지다
grammar.explanation_en: Attach -아/어지다 to an adjective to describe a gradual change or shift in state. Think of it as 'it got ___' or 'it became ___'.
grammar.pattern: Adj stem (ㅏ/ㅗ) + -아지다  ·  Adj stem (other) + -어지다  ·  ㅎ-irregular: ㅎ drops → -아/어지다

scene_example.korean: 속이 좀 괜찮아졌어? / 이제 좀 나아진 것 같아.
scene_example.english: Is your stomach any better? / I think I'm starting to feel better now.

rule_boxes.title: Change of state · 상태 변화
rule_boxes.content:
Adjective + 아/어지다 = 'it got ___' / 'it became ___'
좋다 → 좋아지다 (get better)
나쁘다 → 나빠지다 (get worse)
따뜻하다 → 따뜻해지다 (become warm)

rule_boxes.title: Past tense form · 과거형
rule_boxes.content:
-아/어졌어요 = it got ___ (past)
괜찮아졌어요 — she's feeling better now
날씨가 따뜻해졌어요 — the weather has warmed up

rule_boxes.title: ㅎ irregular · ㅎ 불규칙
rule_boxes.content:
ㅎ adjectives drop ㅎ before 아/어:
좋다 → 좋아지다 ✓ (ㅎ없음 — regular)
빨갛다 → 빨개지다 (ㅎ drops)
하얗다 → 하얘지다 (ㅎ drops)

grammar.title: -게 되다
grammar.title_en: To end up doing / It came about that…
grammar.explanation_en: Use -게 되다 when something happens as a natural result of circumstances — not by your direct choice. It's the 'things just worked out this way' grammar.

scene_example.english: We'll be at the rest stop soon.

rule_boxes.title: Natural outcome · 자연스러운 결과
rule_boxes.content:
Verb + -게 되다 = came to / ended up ___-ing
Happened due to circumstances, not personal choice
한국어를 배우게 됐어요.
→ I ended up learning Korean. (circumstances led me here)

rule_boxes.title: Tense · 시제
rule_boxes.content:
-게 됐어요 — it came about (past)
-게 돼요 — it works out (present)
-게 될 거예요 — it'll end up (future)
도착하게 돼 → we're going to end up arriving

rule_boxes.title: vs plain verb · 일반 동사와 비교
rule_boxes.content:
도착해요 = we arrive (simple fact)
도착하게 돼요 = we end up arriving (naturally, given the situation)

친구가 됐어요 = became friends (simple)
친구가 되게 됐어요 = ended up becoming friends (circumstances)

examples.en: I ended up living in Korea.

examples.ko: 그 사람이랑 같은 반이 되게 됐어요.
examples.en: I ended up being in the same class as them.

grammar.title: -는 것 (명사화)
grammar.explanation_en: Turn any verb into a noun by adding -는 것. This is one of the most powerful structures in Korean — it lets you use whole verbs as subjects, objects, or complements.
grammar.pattern: Verb stem + -는 것 (present)  ·  Verb stem + -(으)ㄴ 것 (past/completed)

scene_example.korean: 참는 게 힘들어요. / 멀미를 참는 것이 쉽지 않지. / 나아진 것 같아.
scene_example.english: Holding on is so hard. / Fighting off motion sickness isn't easy. / It seems like I've gotten better.
scene_example.highlight: 참는 게 · 참는 것이 · 나아진 것

rule_boxes.title: Verb → Noun · 동사를 명사로
rule_boxes.content:
Verb + -는 것 = 'the act of ___-ing'
참다 → 참는 것 (holding on / the act of enduring)
먹다 → 먹는 것 (eating / food)
배우다 → 배우는 것 (learning)

rule_boxes.title: Shorthand forms · 줄임말
rule_boxes.content:
-는 것이 → -는 게  (subject)
-는 것을 → -는 걸  (object)

참는 것이 힘들어요 = 참는 게 힘들어요
좋아하는 것을 해요 = 좋아하는 걸 해요

rule_boxes.title: Past state: -(으)ㄴ 것 같다
rule_boxes.content:
For completed actions or past states, use -(으)ㄴ 것:
나아지다 → 나아진 것 같아 = it seems like (I've) gotten better
먹다 → 먹은 것 같아 = it seems like (someone) ate it

Present: 자는 것 같아 (seems to be sleeping)
Past: 잔 것 같아 (seems to have slept)

examples.en: It seems like it's already over.

grammar.title: -지 않다 vs -지 못하다
grammar.title_en: Isn't / Won't vs Can't (two types of negation)
grammar.explanation_en: Korean has two distinct negative patterns: -지 않다 for describing a state or choice ('isn't / doesn't'), and -지 못하다 for inability ('can't'). Mixing them up is one of the most common learner mistakes.
grammar.pattern: Verb/Adj stem + -지 않다  ·  Verb stem + -지 못하다

scene_example.english: Fighting off motion sickness isn't easy.

rule_boxes.title: -지 않다 · Isn't / Doesn't
rule_boxes.content:
Neutral negation — describes a state or a choice
쉽지 않아요 = it isn't easy
먹지 않아요 = (I) don't eat it / (I'm) not eating
오지 않아요 = (she) isn't coming

rule_boxes.title: -지 못하다 · Can't
rule_boxes.content:
Inability — something prevents you from doing it
참지 못하겠어요 = I can't stand it (any longer)
먹지 못해요 = can't eat it (allergy / circumstances)
오지 못해요 = can't make it (something came up)

rule_boxes.title: With adjectives · 형용사에는 않다만
rule_boxes.content:
못하다 is for actions, not states:
✓ 쉽지 않아요 (it isn't easy)
✗ 쉽지 못해요 (awkward — 'can't be easy' makes no sense)

안 vs 못 shorthand:
안 먹어요 = don't eat
못 먹어요 = can't eat

rule_boxes.title: Common mistake · 주의
rule_boxes.content:
❌ 한국어를 잘하지 못 않아요 (double negative)
✓ 한국어를 잘하지 못해요 (can't speak Korean well)
✓ 한국어를 잘하지 않아요 (don't speak Korean well — by choice or habit)

examples.en: I'm not going to school today.

banmal_jondaemal.intro_ko: 이번 에피소드에서 어른과 아이의 말투 차이를 자세히 들어봐요.
banmal_jondaemal.intro_en: Notice how the speech levels shift throughout this episode.

rows.note: 어른이 아이에게는 반말 · Elders use informal speech with children
아이가 어른에게는 존댓말 · Children use formal speech with elders
친한 가족 사이 반말 OK · Casual speech between close family members

banmal_jondaemal.tip: -(으)ㄹ 수 있겠어? is a gentle way to ask 'do you think you can…?' — softer than -(으)ㄹ 수 있어?

adult_dialogue.title: 직장 동료 — 어디 불편하세요? (Workplace — Are you feeling unwell?)

lines.speaker: 동료
lines.text: 오늘 안색이 안 좋아 보이는데, 어디 불편하세요?
lines.en: You don't look so well today — are you feeling alright?

lines.speaker: 나
lines.text: 네, 아침부터 속이 좀 안 좋아졌어요.
lines.en: I've been feeling a bit off since this morning.

lines.speaker: 동료
lines.text: 많이 힘드시면 오늘 일찍 퇴근하셔도 돼요.
lines.en: If it's too much, feel free to head home early today.

lines.speaker: 나
lines.text: 감사해요. 점심 먹고 나서 좀 나아지면 좋겠네요.
lines.en: Thank you. I'm hoping I'll feel better after lunch.

items.note: 참다 + 는 = 참는 — the ㄴ and ㅁ stay distinct. No assimilation here.
쉽 + 지 → [쉽찌] (tensification: plain stop before 지 → tense) · 않 + 지 → [안치] (ㅎ + ㅈ → aspirate ㅊ)
들 + 어 → [드러] — ㄹ following a vowel-final syllable triggers flapping
나아지다 is a regular verb — each vowel is pronounced clearly: 나-아-진. Don't blend into [나진].

usage.intro_ko: -아/어지다, -게 되다, -는 것을 일상에서 어떻게 쓸 수 있을까요?
usage.intro_en: Let's see how these patterns show up in everyday life.

items.ko: 한국어 실력이 많이 늘었어요. 발음도 좋아졌어요!
items.en: My Korean has improved so much. My pronunciation has gotten better too!
The weather has been getting warmer lately.

items.ko: 커피를 마셨더니 피곤함이 좀 풀렸어요. 괜찮아졌어요.
items.en: I had some coffee and it helped. I'm feeling better now.

items.ko: 한국 드라마를 보다가 한국어를 배우게 됐어요.
items.en: I started watching K-dramas and ended up learning Korean.

items.ko: 우연히 그 책을 읽게 됐는데, 인생이 바뀌었어요.
items.en: I happened to read that book, and it changed my life.

items.ko: 이사 오면서 이 동네를 좋아하게 됐어요.
items.en: After moving here, I came to really love this neighborhood.
Working out every day is my goal.

items.ko: 새로운 언어를 배우는 게 생각보다 재미있어요.
items.en: Learning a new language is more fun than I thought.

items.ko: 아무것도 안 하는 것도 때로는 필요해요.
items.en: Sometimes doing nothing at all is exactly what you need.

options: Is your stomach feeling any better?
Do you want to stop the car?
We already passed the rest stop.
Let's stop and get some food.
We'll be at the rest stop soon.
Do you want me to pull over?

quiz.question_en: What grammar pattern is '참는 게' in this sentence?

options: She thinks she's starting to feel a bit better.
She fell asleep in the car.

quiz.question_en: Which negation pattern is used in '쉽지 않지'?

options: A type of Korean shaved ice
Rice cake and sausage skewers with sauce

real_life.title: 🛣️ 한국 고속도로 휴게소 문화
real_life.title_en: Korean Highway Rest Stops — A Culture of Their Own
real_life.content_ko: 한국 고속도로 휴게소는 그냥 화장실 쉬는 곳이 아니에요! 전국 200개가 넘는 휴게소마다 지역 특산물과 다양한 음식을 팔아요. 소떡소떡, 핫도그, 어묵, 호떡, 커피까지 — 드라이브 중에 꼭 들러봐야 하는 곳이에요.
real_life.content_en: Korean highway rest stops (휴게소) are a whole experience. With over 200 stops nationwide, each one offers regional specialties and a lineup of popular snacks. Sotteok-sotteok, Korean corn dogs, fishcake skewers, hotteok, coffee — stopping at a 휴게소 is practically a road trip tradition.

real_life.title: 🍢 소떡소떡 vs 🌭 핫도그
real_life.content_ko:
• 소떡소떡: 소시지(소)+떡(떡) — 꼬치에 꽂아 달콤 매콤한 소스를 발라 구운 간식
• 핫도그: 미국 핫도그가 아니에요! 긴 밀가루 반죽에 소시지나 치즈를 넣고 튀긴 한국식 간식. 설탕을 뿌리기도 해요.
real_life.content_en:
• Sotteok-sotteok: 소(sausage) + 떡(rice cake) on a skewer, grilled with a sweet-and-spicy glaze
• Korean corn dog (핫도그): nothing like the American version — it's a thick batter-fried skewer with sausage or cheese inside, sometimes rolled in sugar. Dangerously good.

messages.text: 나 오늘 차 타고 오다가 멀미해서 진짜 힘들었어.
messages.en: I had the worst time on the way here — got so carsick.
Oh no, seriously? That sounds rough.

messages.text: 응. 근데 휴게소에서 소떡소떡 먹었더니 좀 나아졌어.
messages.en: Yeah. But I had some sotteok-sotteok at the rest stop and it helped a lot.

messages.text: 맞아, 완전 나아진 것 같았어 ㅎㅎ
messages.en: Honestly, I think it worked! Haha.

self_check: 나는 '-아/어지다'로 몸 상태 변화를 표현할 수 있어요. (괜찮아졌어? / 나아진 것 같아)
나는 '-게 되다'로 자연스럽게 일어난 상황을 말할 수 있어요. (도착하게 돼)
나는 '참는 것이 힘들다'처럼 동사를 명사로 만들어 쓸 수 있어요.
한국 고속도로 휴게소와 소떡소떡이 뭔지 친구에게 설명할 수 있어요.
